---
title: "AKI Classification"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
css: styles.css
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{AKI Classification}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r set-defaults, echo=FALSE, results=FALSE, message=FALSE}
source("knitr_setup.R")
```

This article shows how Metabodecon can be used for deconvolution and alignment
in a supervised classification task on the AKI dataset. The AKI dataset consists
of 1D-NMR urine spectra from patients with acute kidney injury collected 24 h
after surgery in Erlangen. Phenodata can be obtained with
`download_aki_dataset()`, while the raw spectra are available on `r4` at
`/data/aki/AKI-Studie_Urin_Erlangen/` for authorized users.

# Estimation of End-to-End Performance through Classification

To estimate the end-to-end performance of metabodecon in comparison to other,
established methods, we evaluated its deconvolution and alignment workflow
against three commonly used preprocessing strategies in a supervised
classification setting: equidistant binning, wavelet-based peak detection and
alignment using speaq, and the automated peak picking and alignment procedure
implemented in Bruker TopSpin. Equidistant binning represents a widely adopted
and robust baseline in NMR metabolomics (Emwas et al., 2019), while
wavelet-based peak detection has been shown to improve peak localization and
noise robustness compared to fixed binning approaches (Vu et al., 2011; Vu et
al., 2019). Vendor-provided pipelines such as TopSpin reflect common analytical
practice and therefore constitute a relevant real-world reference. Each
preprocessing method yielded a feature matrix of aligned chemical shift
positions with corresponding peak integrals or binned intensities. To assess
discriminative performance between AKI patients and controls, we trained
logistic regression models with an elastic-net penalty, which is well suited for
high-dimensional, correlated metabolomics data with limited sample size (Zou &
Hastie, 2005; Kirpich et al., 2018). Model hyperparameters (regularization
strength and mixing parameter) were tuned using nested cross-validation, and
predictive performance was quantified by balanced accuracy and area under the
ROC curve (AUROC), with feature scaling performed within each training fold to
prevent information leakage. This evaluation framework ensures an unbiased
comparison of preprocessing strategies and quantifies their full end-to-end
discriminative performance.


<!-- Footnotes -->
